%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# 3.13 Summary — LTID Time-Domain Analysis

> **Section Objective:** Review the core time-domain tools used to analyze linear, time-invariant, discrete-time systems.

---

## Concepts In This Section

- LTID difference equations
- Auxiliary conditions
- Characteristic modes
- Unit impulse
- Impulse response
- Zero-state response
- Convolution sum
- Everlasting exponential input

## 📌 Key Takeaways

An LTID system described by an Nth-order difference equation requires exactly **N auxiliary conditions** to produce a unique solution. Its natural response is built by combining characteristic modes.

Here, \(\gamma\) is a **characteristic root** — a value satisfying the system's characteristic equation — and \(\gamma^n\) is the corresponding discrete-time exponential mode.

**When to use:** The root appears exactly once (unrepeated).

**Exam trigger:** A single root listed in the characteristic equation.

**Common misuse:** Do not confuse \(\gamma^n\) with the continuous-time form \(e^{st}\). The independent variable \(n\) is an integer, not a continuous time.

**Minimal example:** Root \(\gamma = 0.5\) → mode is \((0.5)^n\).

$$\gamma \;\longrightarrow\; \gamma^n$$

$$\gamma \text{ (repeated)} \;\longrightarrow\; n^i\gamma^n$$
*When the same root \(\gamma\) appears more than once, the mode is **not** simply another copy of \(\gamma^n\). Instead, it gains polynomial powers of \(n\).

Here, \(i\) is a nonnegative integer index: \(i = 0, 1, 2, \ldots\) up to one less than the multiplicity.

**When to use:** A root has multiplicity greater than 1.

**Exam trigger:** A root listed with a repeated factor in the characteristic polynomial.

**Common misuse:** Writing only \(\gamma^n\) and forgetting the extra \(n^i\) factors — this misses independent modes and gives a wrong general solution.

**Minimal example:** A double root \(\gamma\) produces two modes: \(\gamma^n\) (for \(i=0\)) and \(n\gamma^n\) (for \(i=1\)).*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

## 2. The unit impulse is the building block

\(\delta[n]\) is a sequence with exactly **one nonzero sample**: value 1 at \(n=0\), and zero everywhere else.

LTID analysis uses **shifted impulses** to decompose a general input into simple pieces. Each shifted impulse \(\delta[n-k]\) is a copy of \(\delta[n]\) moved to position \(k\).

The **unit impulse response** \(h[n]\) is the system output when the input is \(\delta[n]\). Once \(h[n]\) is known, the response to any input can be computed.

**When to use:** Problems involving impulse response, zero-state response, or convolution.

**Exam trigger:** Phrases like 'unit impulse response' or 'input decomposed into impulses.'

**Common misuse:** Thinking \(\delta[n]\) is a tall continuous spike. In discrete time it is simply one sample with value 1.

$$\delta[n]=\begin{cases}1, & n=0\\0, & n\neq 0\end{cases}$$

## 3. Zero-state response comes from the convolution sum

\(y_{zs}[n]\) is the part of the output caused **only by the external input**, assuming no stored initial energy (zero initial conditions).

Here \(x[n]\) is the input sequence and \(h[n]\) is the impulse response. The \(*\) symbol denotes convolution, not pointwise multiplication.

**When to use:** The system is LTID and the impulse response is known; find the response to an arbitrary input.

**Exam trigger:** 'Find the response to arbitrary input using \(h[n]\).'

**Common misuse:** Using convolution for the **total** response without checking whether a zero-input response from auxiliary conditions must also be added.

$$y_{zs}[n] = x[n] * h[n]$$

$$y_{zs}[n] = \sum_k x[k]\, h[n-k]$$
*This is the **standard convolution-sum structure**. The exact summation limits depend on where \(x[k]\) and \(h[k]\) are nonzero.

- \(k\) — dummy summation index
- \(x[k]\) — the weight (amplitude) of the impulse component at time \(k\)
- \(h[n-k]\) — the shifted impulse response contribution observed at output time \(n\)

At each output time \(n\), only the overlapping nonzero samples of \(x[k]\) and \(h[n-k]\) contribute.

**When to use:** Input is decomposed into shifted impulses; system is linear and time-invariant.

**Exam trigger:** A table or stem plot of \(x[n]\) and \(h[n]\) is given.

**Common misuse:** Writing \(h[k-n]\) instead of \(h[n-k]\) — this reverses the flip/shift pattern and gives wrong results.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="example_support" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

## 4. Everlasting exponentials are special inputs

For an LTID system, an **everlasting exponential input** \(z^n\) produces an output with the **same exponential shape**, multiplied by a constant.

- \(z\) — the exponential base (may be complex)
- \(n\) — the integer time index
- \(C(z)\) — a multiplicative constant that depends on the system and on \(z\)

This is the discrete-time eigenfunction property: the system cannot change the shape of \(z^n\), only its scale.

**When to use:** The input is an exponential that exists for all relevant integer times (everlasting).

**Exam trigger:** 'Response to \(z^n\)' or 'exponential input.'

**Common misuse:** Assuming this applies automatically to a finite-duration or one-sided exponential without verifying that the everlasting condition holds.

$$z^n \;\longrightarrow\; C(z)\,z^n$$

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- An Nth-order LTID difference equation requires **N auxiliary conditions** for a unique solution.
- Unrepeated root rule: \(\gamma \to \gamma^n\) — the root is the base, the integer time index is the exponent.
- Repeated root rule: \(\gamma \to n^i\gamma^n\) — polynomial powers of \(n\) multiply \(\gamma^n\) for each repeated occurrence.
- Unit impulse: \(\delta[n] = 1\) at \(n=0\), and \(\delta[n] = 0\) for \(n \neq 0\) — one nonzero sample only.
- Impulse response \(h[n]\) is the system output when the input is \(\delta[n]\); knowing \(h[n]\) is enough to compute the zero-state response to any input.
- Zero-state response (compact form): \(y_{zs}[n] = x[n] * h[n]\)
- Zero-state response (sum form): \(y_{zs}[n] = \sum_k x[k]\,h[n-k]\) — each input sample weights a shifted copy of \(h[n]\).
- Everlasting exponential eigenfunction property: \(z^n \to C(z)\,z^n\) — same shape, scaled by a system-dependent constant.
- Convolution trap: \(h[n-k]\) and \(h[k-n]\) are not the same — reversing the index changes the flip/shift pattern.

*Next, these ideas become easier to compute when we move from time-domain sums to transform-domain tools.*
